title: The Case for Decentralised Stablecoins under MiCA: A Legal and Policy Analysis
abstract: A comprehensive analysis of how USD-pegged foreign stablecoins issued by non-EU entities — specifically Tether USDt — can continue to operate in the European Union under MiCA while preserving financial stability. Argues for a "Third Country Issuer" equivalence framework distinct from EMT/ART classification.

title: UK Stablecoin Regime Design: Lessons from the US GENIUS Act
abstract: Comparative analysis of the UK FCA/BoE stablecoin regime under development and the US GENIUS Act. Identifies regulatory arbitrage risks and proposes an "outcomes equivalence" model for foreign issuers including Tether, avoiding mandatory local entity requirements.

title: DABA Phase 2 and the 51% Bank Equity Rule: Implications for Global Stablecoin Issuers
abstract: Detailed legislative analysis of South Korea's Digital Asset Basic Act Phase 2 draft provisions, focusing on the proposed 51% bank equity requirement for stablecoin issuers. Argues the rule creates a de facto barrier to foreign issuers and proposes an alternative regulatory model drawing on EU MiCA.

title: Decentralised AI and Blockchain: Navigating the EU AI Act for Autonomous On-Chain Agents
abstract: Examination of how the EU AI Act's definition of "high-risk AI" interacts with autonomous agents operating on public blockchains such as TRON. Proposes a "DeAI Safe Harbour" framework for fully decentralised AI agents that do not involve a single identifiable deployer, drawing on DCGG's engagement with the EU AI Office.

title: CSAM and End-to-End Encryption: Why Chat Control Threatens the Infrastructure of Digital Finance
abstract: Analysis of the EU Commission's ProtectEU strategy and the CSAM/Chat Control trilogue, focusing on the existential threat to hardware wallet encryption and secure key management. Argues that mandatory client-side scanning requirements would undermine the technical foundations of self-custody solutions like Ledger.

title: Brazil's BCB VASP Authorization Framework: A Model for Emerging Markets Stablecoin Regulation
abstract: Assessment of Brazil's new Banco Central do Brasil authorization regime for VASPs, effective February 2026. Examines the cross-border stablecoin treatment as "foreign exchange operations" and its implications for Tether's operational model in Latin America, with recommendations drawn from DCGG's Consultations 109/110/111 filings.

title: The UAE Regulatory Archipelago: Navigating VARA, ADGM FSRA, DFSA and CBUAE for Digital Asset Firms
abstract: Comprehensive practitioner guide to the UAE's multi-jurisdictional regulatory landscape for digital asset firms. Analyses the division of competence between VARA (Dubai mainland), DFSA (DIFC), ADGM FSRA (Abu Dhabi), and CBUAE at the federal level. Includes an entity mapping tool for stablecoin issuers, Layer 1 blockchains, and hardware wallet providers.

title: Africa's Crypto Regulatory Convergence: ISA 2025, CBK Kenya, and FSCA South Africa in Comparative Perspective
abstract: Comparative regulatory analysis across Nigeria (ISA 2025), Kenya (CBK VASP Draft SI), and South Africa (FSCA CASP licensing). Maps the regulatory treatment of USD stablecoins across all three jurisdictions and identifies the African Union harmonisation agenda as a strategic opportunity for DCGG member engagement.

title: Hong Kong's Stablecoins Ordinance and the OTC Dealer Question: A Win for USD Stablecoin Access
abstract: Analysis of the Hong Kong Stablecoins Ordinance (effective August 2025) and FSTB/SFC guidance permitting licensed OTC dealers to offer USDT to professional investors. Documents the policy win secured by DCGG and outlines next steps for first-mover stablecoin licensing under the HKMA framework.

title: MiCA Article 70 and the Non-Custodial Exemption: Protecting Self-Custody Hardware Wallets
abstract: Analysis of MiCA Article 70 and its application to hardware wallet manufacturers that do not hold customer private keys. Argues that Ledger and similar non-custodial providers are categorically excluded from MiCA's CASP licensing requirements and should not face indirect regulatory burden through ESMA technical standards.
